cases.name: Product sense answer uses JTBD framing before solutions
cases.user_message: How would you improve Instagram Stories?

criteria.description: Response identifies a specific user segment and their problem or job-to-be-done before proposing any solution
Response does NOT jump directly to feature ideas without first framing the user and their problem
Response includes success metrics or a way to measure whether the improvement worked
Response names at least one tradeoff or downside of the proposed approach

cases.name: Execution question elicits structured prioritization with a decision
cases.user_message: You have three features your stakeholders want shipped this quarter, but engineering capacity for only one. How do you decide?

criteria.description: Response names explicit decision criteria or a prioritization framework (e.g., impact, effort, strategic alignment, urgency)
Response makes a clear recommendation or decision rather than leaving the choice open-ended
Response addresses stakeholder communication — how to inform stakeholders whose features are deprioritized

cases.name: Estimation answer uses structured decomposition
cases.user_message: How many Uber rides happen in New York City per day?

criteria.description: Response breaks the estimate into independent component variables rather than guessing a single number
Response states assumptions explicitly for each component of the estimate
Response includes a sanity check — comparing the final number against a known reference point or gut-checking reasonableness
Response identifies which variable has the most sensitivity — which assumption, if wrong, changes the answer the most

cases.name: Behavioral coaching enforces STAR with PM depth
cases.user_message: Tell me about a time you had to make a product decision with incomplete data. Here's my answer: We were deciding whether to launch a new feature. We didn't have much data so we just went with our gut and launched it. It worked out well and users liked it.

criteria.description: Response flags that the answer lacks specificity — no concrete situation, no quantified result, no decision rationale
Response coaches the user to include specific metrics or quantified outcomes in the Result section
Response identifies the missing Action depth — the answer doesn't explain what the candidate personally did, what alternatives were considered, or why this approach was chosen
Response does NOT praise the answer as strong or sufficient without significant critique

cases.name: Mock interview mode activates with proper setup and scoring
cases.user_message: Can you run a mock PM interview with me? I'm preparing for a Google PM interview.

criteria.description: Response asks clarifying setup questions before starting — such as question type (product sense, execution, estimation, behavioral), session length, or focus area
Response does NOT immediately launch into a question without any setup or context gathering
Response acknowledges the Google context and calibrates expectations or approach accordingly (e.g., mentioning scale, data-driven rigor, or structured frameworks)

cases.name: Anti-pattern detection flags solution-first thinking
cases.user_message: For the question 'How would you improve Spotify?', here's my answer: I'd add a feature where users can create collaborative playlists with friends in real-time, with a shared queue and voting on the next song. We could also add AI-generated playlist covers and social listening rooms.

criteria.description: Response flags the solution-first anti-pattern — the candidate jumped to features without identifying a user, a problem, or a job-to-be-done
Response flags the breadth-without-depth anti-pattern — multiple feature ideas listed without going deep on any single one
Response coaches the user to start with a specific user segment and their problem before proposing solutions
Response notes the absence of success metrics or tradeoffs in the answer
